title: Catha dryandri
intro: Catha edulis, also known as Catha dryandri, is a flowering plant native to the Horn of Africa and the Arabian Peninsula, belonging to the family Celastraceae. The plant is known for its stimulant and nootropic properties, providing health benefits such as improved cognitive function, increased alertness, and enhanced mental clarity. The bioactive constituents of Catha edulis include alkaloids, particularly cathinone and cathine, as well as flavonoids and phenolic acids. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and chewing sticks, often used in traditional medicine for its stimulating and medicinal properties.
taxonomy: Catha dryandri is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae. It belongs to the genus Catha, which is native to tropical Africa. The plant is also known as the Ceylon myrrh tree or the myrrh tree, and it is a shrub or small tree that grows up to 10 meters in height. The classification of Catha dryandri is further divided into several sub-species, each with unique characteristics and growth habits. This plant is valued for its aromatic resin, which is used in traditional medicine and perfumery.
uses: Catha dryandri, a plant native to the tropical regions of Africa and Asia, is believed to have various medicinal properties. side_effects: Catha dryandri, also known as black catechu, has been associated with several potential side effects. These may include gastrointestinal issues such as nausea and vomiting, as well as allergic reactions like skin irritation and respiratory problems. Additionally, excessive consumption of black catechu may lead to liver damage and other organ toxicity.
